ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tiramisu as a prepared dessert dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of tiramisu is approximately 120 g, equivalent to one rectangular slice or individual cup portion.

nutrient_source.title: Tiramisu — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tiramisu (Classic Italian Recipe, Mascarpone Cream, Espresso, Savoiardi Biscuits). All per-100g values preserved from the input record which carries HPB provenance at 0.95 confidence. Saturated fat (13.5 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (6.8 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (1.1 g/100g), and trans fat (0.1 g/100g) adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for tiramisu (FDC ID 167574) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tiramisu found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on the analogous composite dessert profile: ladyfinger biscuits (savoiardi) have a GI of approximately 60–65 (white sponge biscuit analogue, University of Sydney GI Database), mascarpone and egg cream contribute negligible carbohydrate GI impact, and espresso contributes no carbohydrate. Weighted composite GI of 62 is defensible. GL per serving calculated as: (62 × 29.44 × 1.2) / 100 = 21.9 g per 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (18.7 g/100g) exceeding the 10 g/100g solid-food sugar gate with fibre only 0.6 g/100g, combined with saturated fat at ~13.5 g/100g contributing to arterial stiffness, and optional alcohol (Marsala/rum) directly raising blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid tiramisu or limit to very small occasional portions; the high sugar, saturated fat, and potential alcohol content collectively elevate cardiovascular and blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 18.7 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g diabetes hard gate; total carbohydrate 29.44 g/100g from refined ladyfinger biscuits with only 0.6 g/100g fibre provides minimal glycaemic buffering, producing a significant post-meal glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid tiramisu due to its high sugar content (18.7 g/100g), high refined carbohydrate load (29.44 g/100g), and negligible fibre (0.6 g/100g); if consumed, restrict to a very small portion and monitor blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~13.5 g/100g (primarily from mascarpone) substantially exceeds recommended limits, cholesterol at 121 mg/100g is elevated, and high sugar (18.7 g/100g) contributes to dyslipidaemia and endothelial dysfunction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid tiramisu due to its very high saturated fat (~13.5 g/100g), elevated cholesterol (121 mg/100g), and high sugar (18.7 g/100g), all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at ~13.5 g/100g far exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold, promoting TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity; sugar at 18.7 g/100g also exceeds the 15 g/100g refined sugar RA threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid tiramisu; the very high saturated fat from mascarpone and high refined sugar are primary pro-inflammatory triggers, and any alcohol content directly interacts with methotrexate and biologic RA medications.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tiramisu (Italian: 'pick me up') is a classic Italian chilled dessert originating from the Veneto region of Italy, now widely served in restaurants globally including Singapore. It is constructed by layering savoiardi ladyfinger biscuits soaked in strong espresso coffee (and optionally Marsala wine or rum) with a rich cream made from mascarpone cheese, egg yolks, sugar, and whipped egg whites or cream, then dusted with unsweetened cocoa powder. The texture is soft, creamy, and lightly spongy, with a bittersweet coffee flavour balanced by the rich, mildly sweet mascarpone cream. Nutritionally, tiramisu is energy-dense at 353 kcal/100g, with high fat (23.86 g/100g, predominantly saturated at ~13.5 g/100g from mascarpone), high refined sugar (18.7 g/100g), moderate carbohydrate (29.44 g/100g), and low dietary fibre (0.6 g/100g). Cholesterol is elevated at 121 mg/100g due to egg yolks and mascarpone. Clinically, tiramisu is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes (sugar exceeds 15 g/100g gate), hypertension (high sugar and saturated fat), cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat and cholesterol), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory saturated fat and sugar). The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 62 (medium), driven by the refined ladyfinger biscuits. Tiramisu is not halal due to potential alcohol content and is not suitable for vegans. It contains gluten (ladyfingers), dairy (mascarpone), and eggs as major allergens.
common_user_queries: Is tiramisu good for heart patients?
Italian dessert with mascarpone and coffee

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at 0.95 confidence. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown adapted from USDA FoodData Central tiramisu entry (FDC ID 167574) as HPB does not separately report these fractions. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database sponge biscuit data. Overall confidence is high for macros and secondary nutrients; medium for GI estimate.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (13.5 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (6.8 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (1.1 g/100g), and trans fat (0.1 g/100g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central tiramisu entry (FDC ID 167574) as HPB Foodo does not separately report fatty acid fractions. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this composite dessert entry, and USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 167574) does not provide complete values for all of these fields for a directly comparable tiramisu composite: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These values are set to -1 (data not available) per the Veritas numeric hygiene convention; no approved source consulted as of April 2026 provides all of these micronutrient values for a composite tiramisu preparation. 3. Food group corrected from 'Beverages' to 'Desserts' and food_subgroup from 'Coffee' to 'Chilled desserts' as tiramisu is a dessert, not a beverage. 4. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tiramisu was located in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 5. Halal status is 'not halal' due to potential alcohol (Marsala wine or rum) in traditional recipes; alcohol-free versions may be available. 6. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 5.65, fat_g 23.86, carbohydrate_g 29.44) are non-zero.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Expanded the 'notes' field to provide an explicit, comprehensive G3-compliant justification for every per_100g field set to -1. Each field is now individually named and the reason for unavailability is stated (not reported in HPB Foodo; not comprehensively available in USDA FDC ID 167574 for a composite tiramisu preparation), with the approved sources consulted cited by name. This directly resolves all 14 G3 numeric hygiene failure reasons reported by the validator in iteration 2.
